description:
The "aria-hidden" attribute is provided to "SVK" image button present in the footer section. As a result, screen reader users cannot access the image button effectively.

Code Snippet:
<button title="back to top" class="back-to-top" aria-hidden="true" type="button">(...)</button>
rationale: The aria-hidden="true" attribute is used to hide the content from assistive technology users. Although it is a best practice to use the aria-hidden="true" attribute to hide irrelevant content for screen reader users, it can pose issues for screen reader users if it is not used correctly. When aria-hidden is set to "true" for an element on a web page, screen readers will not identify the element for their users, even though visually the element is present on the screen.
suggestion:
Remove aria-hidden=true from the <button> element in the page source.

<button title="back to top" class="back-to-top"  type="button">(...)</button>

issue: ARIA Role and state defined inappropriately
description:
The role tab" is defined to
elements containing the accordion tabs "SVK", "SVK" etc. inappropriately. Additionally, role "button" is provided in the source code of the accordion tabs. As a result, the screen reader (JAWS) announces "button" and the screen reader (NVDA) announces "tab" and "button" for the accordion tab. Moreover, "aria-expanded" attribute is not defined in the source code of the accordion tabs by default. As a result, screen reader users will not understand the state of the accordion tabs by default. Code Snippet:

How do I cancel an existing appointment?
rationale:
All the interactive elements must be accessible for assistive technology users such as screen reader uses. When native HTML elements are used basic information about an element, such as name, role, state and value is exposed to assistive technologies by default. However, for custom components this information needs to be made available for assistive technologies so users can interact with the custom components effectively.
When role and state of an interactive element are not exposed to assistive technologies appropriately through code, users might not be able to interact with the element effectively.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:  Remove role tab" from the div element containing the accordion tabs.  Remove role "button" from the element and define role "tab" to element of the accordion tabs.  Set "aria-expanded" attribute to "false" to the accordion tabs by default. Ensure that the value of "aria-expanded" attribute gets updated as per user interaction via scripting.

How do I cancel an existing appointment?

When appropriate role information is not specified accurately for a web page element, it becomes confusing for assistive technology users (especially screen reader users) and in some cases even difficult for users to interact with the functionality associated with the element.
Screen reader renders the content from the mark-up and present the functionality to its users in the way that users can understand. If appropriate mark-up is not used to represent the content, it may pose problems, and thereby make the content difficult to interpret for screen reader users.

description: An alternative for the content displayed within video "SVK" is missing. This resulted in users with visual impairments as well as deaf-blind users not being able to access the information conveyed in the video.
rationale:
Text transcript is full textual description of the video including audio and visual content. Text transcript must be accurate and displayed on user's request. Users can click a button to access the transcript. Text transcripts are helpful for users with hearing impairments, deaf-blind users, and low bandwidth users and also preferred by visually impaired users.
Audio track is added for video only content to include narration of the information conveyed visually. It should describe any important visual content of the video. In case of videos with no audio, adding an audio track helps visually impaired users understand the video content by listening to the audiotrack.
suggestion:
Provide information using one of the alternative methods given below for the discussed video:
 Provide text transcripts for the video and ensure that the description is accurate. The text transcript can be displayed on a separate page and a link/button can be provided besides the video to help users access the transcript. This will ensure that the video content is accessible for users with visual impairments and as well as deaf-blind users. 
 Include the information in the form of an audio track that can be added to the video. This will ensure that the video content is accessible for users with visual impairments.

description: Captions are missing for the videos which become visible when user clicks on the "SVK" and "SVK" buttons below SVK, SVK, SVK, etc sections. In the absence of captions, the audio content of the video will be inaccessible for users with hearing impairments.
rationale:
Captions are textual description of the audio content of a synchronized video. Captions are generally displayed at the bottom of the screen. Captions are different from sub-titles as sub-titles include textual description of the spoken word whereas captions include description for all the audio content including background sounds. 
Captions are mainly of two types, Open captions and Close captions. It is a good practice to provide Close captions and provide users with an option to access if required. Captions must be synchronized with the video to help users access the content effectively and enjoy the experience.
Captions are provided to help users with hearing impairments access the audio information of a video. Since users with hearing impairments cannot listen to the audio, they rely on captions to understand the audio content. Captions when synchronized with the video helps users watch the video and read the caption text to understand the audio
suggestion: Add synchronized captions for all videos whose audio content communicates information not included in the visual component.

issue: Missing label for video player control
description: The video player (that becomes available on clicking "SVK" button) on the homepage contains SVK slider that lacks textual description. Blind users will be unable to identify the purpose of the control and interact with the video player.
rationale:
Each form control should have a unique label. In the absence of label, it becomes difficult for users especially screen reader users to understand the purpose of the form control and interact with the form control. In the case of form controls, where it is not possible to specify a label, include a description via either the 'title' attribute or via aria-label property. 
Title attribute or aria-label can be specified for form input fields where it is not possible to assign labels due to web page layout and design. WCAG 2.0 allows authors to specify descriptive title attributes or aria-label property for form controls for which labels cannot be assigned. This will help to make the purpose of form controls meaningful for screen reader users.
suggestion: Add descriptive label as "SVK" for the Volume slider via aria-label attribute.

In the absence of text transcript, the audio/video content of the video will be inaccessible for users with visual and hearing impairments (deaf-blind users).
rationale: Text transcript is full textual description of the video including audio and visual content. Text transcript must be accurate and displayed on users request. Users can click a button to access the transcript. Text transcripts are helpful for users with hearing impairments, deaf-blind users, and low bandwidth users and also preferred by visually impaired users.
suggestion: Provide text transcripts for the video and ensure that the description is accurate. The text transcript can be displayed on a separate page and a link/button can be provided besides the video to help users access the transcript.

description: Audio description has not been provided for videos that become available when user clicks on the "SVK" and "SVK" buttons below SVK, SVK, SVK, etc sections. Thus, users with visual impairments could not understand the important visual content conveyed in the video.
rationale: Audio description is a narration of the information conveyed visually. It is a part of the soundtrack and describes any important visual content of the video. Audio description helps visually impaired users understand the video content when listening to the soundtrack.
suggestion: Add narrations in the videos wherever the visual content is conveyed to ensure that users with visual impairments and learning impairments can listen to the narration and understand the content.

issue: Color alone used to convey information
description:
On the page, the "SVK (#SVK)", "SVK (#SVK )", etc. colors are used to identify "SVK", "SVK" and "SVK" dates in the calendar.
Users with color deficiencies will not be able to differentiate between the colors and will have no way to easily identify the change of color in the page.
This information is not available to screen reader users either.
rationale:
Users with visual impairments, such as color-blind users and blind users will find it difficult to understand information that is conveyed using color alone. When color is used to convey important information, it is essential that information is also available in at least one additional form, such as patterns, symbols, text etc. 
Information conveyed with the help of color is easy to understand for people with learning disabilities. However, if color alone is used people with visual impairments will find it difficult to understand the information. Thus, it is essential to supplement color with symbols, text, patterns etc.
suggestion: In the mentioned example along with color, use additional visual clues (e.g. different patterns) to show the states of the dates.

issue: Color alone used to distinguish links
description: Color alone #SVK is used to distinguish "SVK" and "SVK" link from the surrounding text #SVK on the SVK > SVK. Even though sufficient color contrast is provided the links does not have persistent underline making it difficult for users with color blindness to distinguish the link within the running text.
rationale: Users with color deficiencies may not be able to quickly identify links on the website. This is especially true for links displayed within paragraphs when color alone is used to differentiate links from the surrounding text. Therefore it is essential to underline links persistently or make sure that the underline appears when the links receive keyboard focus.
suggestion: To make it easy for all users to recognize links the best practice is to persistently underline them. If the above solution is not feasible, change the color of links so that they have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 with the surrounding text. Moreover, ensure that the underline appears not only when the mouse is hovered over the links but also when the links receive keyboard focus.

description:
Some background/foreground color combinations with an insufficient contrast ratio were found on the page: 
 For the #SVK SVK text "SVK" button has insufficient color contrast of 3.4:1  with #SVK SVK background.
 For the #SVK SVK text "SVK", "SVK" etc. under "SVK" on Expand state has insufficient color contrast of 1.5:1 with #SVK SVK background.
 For the #SVK SVK "SVK" button (that becomes available when user checks the checkbox) "SVK" has insufficient color contrast of 3.4:1  with #SVK SVK background.
 For the #SVK SVK text "SVK" and "SVK" links has insufficient color contrast of 3.4:1  with #SVK SVK background.
 For the #SVK SVK - SVK text "SVK" has insufficient color contrast of 3.3:1  with #SVK SVK background.
 For the #SVK SVK - SVK text "SVK" has insufficient color contrast of 3.4:1  with #SVK SVK background.
rationale: Two colors are considered to have good visibility if the contrast ratio between foreground text and background is sufficient. W3C has described the minimum contrast ratio for text that is less than 18 point (if not bold) and less than 14 point (if bold) to be 4.5:1 at Level AA and 7:1 at Level AAA.
suggestion: To make it easy for all users to recognize links the best practice is to persistently underline them. If the above solution is not feasible, change the color of links so that they have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 with the surrounding text. Moreover, ensure that the underline appears not only when the mouse is hovered over the links but also when the links receive keyboard focus.

issue: Invisible content read by screen reader
description: On activating the "SVK" link in SVK section > "SVK" link in the modal dialog, a modal dialog gets displayed. The keyboard focus moves on the modal dialog content. However, the hidden background content is read by the screen reader when users access the content using arrow keys.
rationale: When hidden content is read out by screen readers, users will get confused and might find the information irrelevant as they have not tried to access the hidden content.
suggestion: Use aria-hidden=true to hide the background content from the screen reader. Ensure that "aria-hidden" attribute is removed from the page source when user closes the modal dialog.

issue: No means to control slideshow content
description: A slideshow was present in the main content area of the Homepage, but no means was provided for users to Stop/Pause and Play the slideshow.
rationale: Slideshow content is often used on web pages to attract users attention. However, users should be provided with an option to control the movement as it might be found distracting by users with learning impairments. If moving content and static content are provided, people with attention deficit disorders find it difficult to concentrate while reading the static content of the page.
suggestion: Provide users with an option to control the slideshow through start, stop and pause buttons or links. Also inform screen reader users in the form of instruction about the presence of slideshow in advance to help them access the web page with ease.

description: On the SVK page, the session expires automatically and the user is taken to the SVK page after a period of time.
rationale:
People with disabilities and users of assistive technology take longer to complete on-line tasks, in particular the completion of forms.
Failure to inform users in advance and not allowing users to increase the time may result in the user having insufficient time to complete the task, rendering the service inaccessible.
suggestion:
We recommend that one of the following techniques be adopted:
1. Turn off time limit  provide a mechanism, e.g. a button, to allow the user to turn off the time limit before encountering it (place the mechanism at the top of the page).
2. Adjust time limit  provide the ability to adjust the set time limit. Allow the user to set the time limit to at least ten times the length of the default setting. 
3. Extend time limit  allow the user to extend their session before the time expires. Warn the user before the time expires and allow the user to simply extend the time (for example, by activating a link or button). Allow the user to extend the time limit at least ten times.

issue: Dynamically updating content not announced for screen reader users
description: As soon as users start typing in "SVK" input field (that becomes available on selecting  "SVK" checkbox) in "SVK" section (that becomes available after logging in), the value of the characters left "SVK" gets automatically updated below. Although this information is easily available for sighted users, it is not announced for blind and low vision users.
rationale:
When content gets updated dynamically on a page, it is essential to make the information available for all users including assistive technology users. Assistive technologies, such as screen readers access web page information in a linear order as it has been included in the page source and any content that gets dynamically updated on the page is not rendered by screen readers. 
Dynamically updating information often goes unnoticed by users with visual impairments. If dynamic updates are being made  to the content, it is necessary to make it available for screen reader users in order to ensure that they do not miss out on important updates.
suggestion:
Ensure that screen reader users are aware of the information that is updated below while they are typing and are able to access the information.
Use appropriate ARIA Live region attributes to achieve this. Specify aria-live = polite to the containner used to display the number of characters left information. This will ensure that each time the change in the number is announced for screen reader users when they are idle.

description: "SVK" button displays sub-menu options such as "SVK" and "SVK" when users hover the mouse on the button. However, the sub-menu options are not accessible for keyboard-only and screen reader users.
rationale: All the page functionality should be accessible with different input devices used by the users. Users should be able to access the page functionality with pointing device, such as a mouse, a keyboard or any other input device. Users with visual impairments and mobility impairments use a keyboard to access the internet. Lack of keyboard access makes it difficult for users with visual and mobility impairments to access web page functionality.
suggestion:
Ensure that all the page functionality is available for all users irrespective of the device being used.
Apply the following changes:
 Modify the scripts associated with "SVK" button to ensure that the sub-menu links are expanded/ collapsed below when user clicks "SVK" button and the sub-menu links are accessible for keyboard-only and screen reader users.
 Ensure that "aria-expanded" attribute is set to "true" in the source code of "SVK" button when user clicks the button via scripting. The value of "aria-expanded" attribute must be updated as per user interaction.
Alternatively, implement the menu functionality by using appropriate ARIA roles, states and properties. Working examples and information on implementing accessible ARIA menus can be found at https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria-practices-1.1/examples/menubar/menubar-1/menubar-1.html

issue: Modal dialog inaccessible for keyboard-only and screen reader users
description:
Activating the "SVK" interactive element triggers a modal dialog. Following issues were found for the modal dialog:
 Even though modal dialog is visible at the center of the screen, modal dialog content is available at the end of the page for keyboard-only and screen reader users. This required in users with mobility and visual impairments who rely on a keyboard to tab pass the content on the page before they can interact with the modal dialog.
 Keyboard focus does not remain within the boundaries of the modal dialog. So, focus moves on the background content when users press "Shift" + "Tab" keys from the first interactive element or "Tab" key from the last interactive element of the modal dialog.
 Screen reader users found it difficult to distinguish between background and the modal dialog content. When screen reader users navigate through the modal dialog using the virtual cursor (up and down arrow keys), the background content is read out thereby creating a confusing experience for the users.
 Keyboard support is missing for the interactive "SVK" (Close) image.
rationale:
Keyboard-only users when interact with a modal dialog, can view the modal dialog on the screen. However, if there focus fails to move to the modal dialog content they are required to tab pass all the interactive functionality of the original page before they can move on the modal dialog. This will result in frustration for keyboard-only users while interacting with the web page. 
When web page content gets updated dynamically, ensure that the users focus moves to the updated section. This will help users access the modal dialog content without requiring to go through the content displayed on the original page from which they accessed the modal dialog.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 Set the keyboard focus to first interactive element such as "SVK" (Close) image button of the modal dialog using the javascript .focus().
 Add role="dialog" and "aria-labelledby" attribute to the <div> element containing the modal dialog content.
 Specify "id" attribute with unique value to the <div> element containing the "SVK" heading and reference it via "aria-labelledby" attribute.
 Ensure that the background content is not available and only the content displayed in the modal dialog is available for keyboard-only and screen reader users:
 Use aria-hidden =true and tabindex="-1" to hide the background content from screen readers. Ensure that "aria-hidden" attribute is removed from  the page source when user closes the modal dialog.
 Modify the scripts to ensure that the Tab and Shift + Tab keystrokes wrap the keyboard focus inside the modal dialog in all browsers.
 Include role="button" and tabindex="0" in the source code of <div> element containing "X" (Close) image.
 Modify the scripts to ensure that the functionality is accessible with a keyboard as well as a mouse.

For information on creating accessible modal dialog refer https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria-practices-1.1/examples/dialog-modal/dialog.html

issue: Missing keyboard support for custom radio buttons
description:
On the SVK form (that becomes available on clicking "SVK" link), the custom rating radio buttons are not accessible via keyboard. This resulted in keyboard-only and screen reader users not being able to interact with the radio buttons.
Similar issue is found with the custom radio buttons (that become available on selecting any option from "SVK" dropdown list).
rationale: All the page functionality should be accessible with different input devices used by the users. Users should be able to access the page functionality with pointing device, such as a mouse, a keyboard or any other input device. Users with visual impairments and mobility impairments use a keyboard to access the internet. Lack of keyboard access makes it difficult for users with visual and mobility impairments to access web page functionality.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 Replace the custom radio buttons with the standard radio buttons by using the <input> element of type="radio". Moreover, provide label as SVK, SVK, etc. for the radio buttons via aria-label attribute. Additionally, use <fieldset> and <legend> elements to programmatically group the radio buttons and associate the radio buttons with "SVK" instruction.

Alternatively, apply the following changes:
 Specify tabindex=0 in the source code of the radio buttons.
 Modify the scripts to ensure that the radio buttons are accessible with a keyboard as well as a mouse.
 Code the custom radio buttons using appropriate ARIA roles, states and properties to enable assistive technology identify the radio button accurately and users can interact with the components easily.
 Add role="radio" for the radio buttons and define appropriate states (aria-checked) and properties to make the radio buttons accessible for screen reader users. Set aria-checked="true" or "false" for each radio button. (Note: the value of aria-checked attribute should change as per user interaction).
 Provide label as SVK, SVK, etc. for the radio buttons via aria-label attribute.
 Add ARIA role "radiogroup" for the container containing all radio buttons.
 Associate the instruction "SVK" with the radio buttons via aria-labelledby attribute.
More information on how to implement this can be found at: https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria-practices-1.1/#radiobutton

issue: Tab functionality Inaccessible for keyboard users
description: The tabs such as SVK, "SVK", "SVK" and "SVK" are inaccessible for keyboard-only and screen reader users. This made it difficult for keyboard-only and screen reader users to access the functionality associated with these tabs.
rationale:
All the interactive elements must be accessible for assistive technology users such as screen reader uses. When native HTML elements are used basic information about an element, such as name, role, state and value is exposed to assistive technologies by default. However, for custom components this information needs to be made available for assistive technologies so users can interact with the custom components effectively.
In the case of tabs, assistive technology should be able to inform their users about the functionality of tabs and the currently selected tab. In addition, as users navigate from one tab to another they must be able to understand that the tab is currently not selected and they need to select it in order to access the information. This will enable users to interact with the tabs effectively.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 Code the tabs using the <a> element and include the href attribute in their source code. 
 Modify the scripts of the tabs to ensure that the tabs are accessible for all users.
 Set the role for the <ul> element containing tabs as "tablist".
 Set the role for the <a> element used for displaying SVK, "SVK", "SVK" and "SVK" as tab. In addition, specify the ARIA attributes for tabs, such as aria-controls and aria-selected. (The value of the aria-selected attribute needs to change on the basis of currently active tab.)
o Set aria-selected=true for the active tab.
o Specify an id attribute for the <div> element used to display the content of each tab. In addition, ensure that the value of the id attribute is referenced correctly via aria-controls attribute of each tab.
 Set the role for the <div> elements displaying the contents associated with SVK, "SVK", "SVK" and "SVK" tabs as tabpanel. 
A working example of accessible tab functionality can be found at https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria-practices-1.1/examples/tabs/tabs-2/tabs.html

issue: Tooltip content inaccessible for keyboard users
description:
Keyboard support is missing for the tooltip content present in the SVK modal dialog (that becomes available when users click on "SVK" link in the main content area). 
This made it difficult for users with mobility and visual impairments to access the tooltip content. 
Additionally, SVK icon (i) is defined through CSS via content property.
rationale: All the page functionality should be accessible with different input devices used by the users. Users should be able to access the page functionality with pointing device, such as a mouse, a keyboard or any other input device. Users with visual impairments and mobility impairments use a keyboard to access the internet. Lack of keyboard access makes it difficult for users with visual and mobility impairments to access web page functionality.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 Provide appropriate textual description via aria-label attribute for the image link.
 Display the information available in the tooltip as plain text and use CSS for formatting.
 Specify the ARIA role "tooltip" for the information displayed on hover. Specify an unique id attribute for the tooltip and reference it via the aria-describedby attribute of the Information image.
 Modify the script to make the tooltip information available on hover as well as on focus. In addition along with mouse specific event handlers, use keyboard specific event handlers, such as "onfocus", "onblur", etc. to make the functionality accessible for keyboard-only users.

description: On activating the carousel controls (that becomes available on clicking "SVK" link) the content of the respective carousel gets dynamically updated. Although the dynamic change in the content is easier to understand for sighted users, it is not intuitive for blind users. As a result, screen reader users cannot interact with the carousel content effectively.
rationale:
The functionality of controls that dynamically add or update content on the page should be indicated to screen reader users. This will enable screen reader users to interact with such controls effectively and access the added or updated content.
When static content is displayed on a page, screen reader can read the information for their users. However, when content is added dynamically, screen readers will not know that there is new content available on the page. In addition, screen readers will not know where the new content is displayed and thus will not be able to inform about the new content to their users.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:  Provide hidden heading Slideshow" and hidden text Slide controls before the carousel content and carousel controls respectively to explain the functionality to screen reader users.  Provide aria-live="polite" for
element containing the added carousel content. HTML:

Slideshow

Shannon Green
Regional Property Director

Shannon oversees properties in the Austin, TX area and has been with Weinstein Properties since 2010.

(...)

CSS: .sr-only { position: absolute; left: -9999px; }

description:
The functionality associated with "SVK" button on the Apartments for Rent page is unclear for screen reader users. Visually, on activating the button, the content gets expanded/collapsed dynamically below on the page. 
Although the dynamic change in the content is easier to understand for sighted users, it is not intuitive for blind users.
rationale: When users click on a button, they expect to move to different page, tab or window or even to a section within a page. If dynamic functionality is associated with links or buttons, ensure that users focus moves to the updated content and inform users about the functionality to orient them about the functionality in advance. Screen reader users need to understand the elements role in order to understand the functionality or purpose of such elements. Screen readers render web page content in a linear order for their users resulting in very often users going unaware about dynamic updates being made to the page.
suggestion: Set the aria-expanded attribute to false for the button when the button is in the collapsed state. Ensure that the value of the aria-expanded attribute changes on user interaction via scripting.

description:
The functionality of the custom tabs (e.g. "SVK" and "SVK") in the main content area of the page is unclear for screen reader users. On activating the tabs, the content gets dynamically updated below in the main content area of the page.
Although the dynamic change in the content is easier to understand for sighted users, it is not intuitive for blind users.
rationale:
All the interactive elements must be accessible for assistive technology users such as screen reader uses. When native HTML elements are used basic information about an element, such as name, role, state and value is exposed to assistive technologies by default. However, for custom components this information needs to be made available for assistive technologies so users can interact with the custom components effectively.
In the case of tabs, assistive technology should be able to inform their users about the functionality of tabs and the currently selected tab. In addition, as users navigate from one tab to another they must be able to understand that the tab is currently not selected and they need to select it in order to access the information. This will enable users to interact with the tabs effectively.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 Set the role for the <div> element containing tabs as "tablist".
 Set the role for the <button> element used for displaying "SVK" and "SVK" tabs as tab. In addition, specify the ARIA attributes for tabs, such as aria-controls and aria-selected. (The value of the aria-selected attribute needs to change on the basis of currently active tab.)
o Set aria-selected=true for the active tab.
o Specify an id attribute for the <button> element used to display the content of each tab. In addition, ensure that the value of the id attribute is referenced correctly via aria-controls attribute of each tab.
 Set the role for the <div> elements displaying the contents associated with "SVK" and "SVK" tabs as tabpanel. 
A working example of accessible tab functionality can be found at Gmail/Inbox.

However, for custom components this information needs to be made available for assistive technologies so users can interact with the custom component effectively.

issue: Date selector widget not keyboard accessible
description: A date selector widget is provided for providing SVK" and SVK dates in book a hotel form on the page. However, the date selector widget cannot be accessed using a keyboard.
rationale:
All the page content and functionality can be accessed using different input/output devices, such as a keyboard or a mouse. Users with mobility and visual impairments rely on a keyboard to interact with the web page functionality. When web page elements, such as date selector widget are not accessible via a keyboard, it becomes difficult for users with mobility and visual impairments to input the date. 
When date selector widget is used, provide users with an option to type the date in a specified format. This can be done by allowing users to type the date manually in an input field.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 If the date field is provided to allow a user to enter a date manually using the keyboard along with the date selector widget, that will be sufficient to satisfy this success criterion. 
 To help screen reader users submit the form successfully, it is recommended to include the instruction about date format within the <label> element of the date field. This text may be hidden off-screen if desired.

Alternatively, use accessible date selector widget that can be accessed by all users including people with mobility and visual impairments. Refer to the following link for example of an accessible date selector widget: link 
http://www.oaa-accessibility.org/examplep/datepicker1/

issue: Error message displayed one at a time
description: On submitting the SVK form with errors, (that becomes available when user clicks on "SVK" button below the sections "SVK" and "SVK"), single error message is displayed at a time. This leads to frustration for users with visual and mobility impairments as each time user rectifies an error and submits the form another error is displayed.
rationale: Error messages serve as a pointer to help users correct their mistakes and submit the form successfully. Error messages should be easy to understand and locate to help users with visual and mobility impairments input the form details accurately. All the fields with error should be displayed together to ensure that all users can access the errors. Displaying one error at a time can frustrate users as they get annoyed when they find out about another error on rectifying the previously displayed error.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 When the form is submitted with errors, display all error messages at the top of the form.
 Set the focus to the first error message in the list using JavaScript focus() method. Alternatively, use a JavaScript dialog box to inform users that error messages are listed at the top of the form.

description: When users submit the SVK form with errors, error messages gets displayed at the beginning of the form and the keyboard focus remains on SVK button itself. As a result, screen reader users were unaware about the occurrence of errors on the page.
rationale:
Error messages or validation messages of forms are pointers that enable users identify the mistakes and rectify them. As a result, error messages should be accurate and descriptive so that users are able to provide correct information and submit the form without any problem.
Error messages and form submission messages must be placed at the beginning of the form to ensure that all users can access them.
Screen reader users are likely to miss the error messages when displayed between form fields. In fact, these users generally tab through form fields without reading any of the text surrounding them (with the exception of labels).
When error messages get dynamically added on or around the form fields, screen readers should be informed about the error being added on the page to ensure that users are aware about the errors.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 Add the 'aria-describedby' attribute to the form field. The value should match id attribute of the error message.
 Set focus on the first field with error using the JavaScript .focus() method as soon as the form is submitted.

description: On the SVK form, the instruction "A field marked with * indicates that the information is required." is inappropriate as the mandatory fields are identified by "**".
rationale:
Screen reader users often prefer identifying mandatory form field information in advance to complete the form successfully and avoid error messages. Well-defined mandatory form fields are also helpful for users with mobility impairments as they can fill up the form with ease. In the absence of mandatory form field information, users might incorrectly fill up the form and thus might lead to frustration. 
The best way to mark mandatory form fields is by including the information within the form labels as all users read the label before filling up form details.
When the instruction regarding mandatory fields is inappropriate, screen reader users may find it difficult to identify the mandatory fields.
suggestion: Modify the instruction as "A field marked with ** indicates that the information is required."

description: There is no indication that the form field  "SVK" is mandatory. Users are informed of the field being mandatory in the form of an error after the input fields of the form are submitted with errors.
rationale:
Screen reader users often prefer identifying mandatory form field information in advance to complete the form successfully and avoid error messages. Well-defined mandatory form fields are also helpful for users with mobility impairments as they can fill up the form with ease. In the absence of mandatory form field information, users might incorrectly fill up the form and thus might lead to frustration. 
The best way to mark mandatory form fields is by including the information within the form labels as all users read the label before filling up the form details.
suggestion: Provide an instruction "Enter at least 1 SVK". Place the instruction at the top of the form field to ensure all users can access the instruction.

description: When the "SVK" form (that becomes available in the modal dialog when user clicks on "SVK" button in the header section) is submitted with errors, the fields with error are identified via a red color rectangular box that gets added for the corresponding field. However, textual error messages are not provided for the error message.
rationale: The lack of textual error messages makes it difficult for users (especially users who are blind or have learning disabilities) to recognize the presence of errors, identify the cause of the problem and understand how to solve it. It is a good practice to display error messages using red color. However, color should not be used as the sole means to convey the error. Along with color, textual error messages should be added to ensure that all users can access the error message.
suggestion: When the form is submitted with errors along with color, display a list of error messages at the top of the form. Error messages must clearly describe the problem that has occurred and include suggestions (wherever possible) on how to rectify it.

description:

rationale:
Labels are specified for form controls to help users understand what information needs to be inputted. However, labels need to be explicitly associated with their respective form controls for assistive technologies to identify the label accurately and convey the information to the users.
Explicitly associating label with its form control benefits people with mobility impairments who find it difficult to click on tiny controls. When labels are explicitly associated with the form control, users can click on the label to select the form control. This makes the clicking area wider for users without affecting the presentation of the page. This association needs to be done programmatically through the code to ensure that assistive technologies can identify the association.

description: Although an asterisk (*) is provided for the mandatory fields in the form , the instruction stating the meaning of the asterisk (*) is not provided.
rationale:
Screen reader users often prefer identifying mandatory form field information in advance to complete the form successfully and avoid error messages. Well-defined mandatory form fields are also helpful for users with mobility impairments as they can fill up the form with ease. In the absence of mandatory form field information, users might incorrectly fill up the form and thus might lead to frustration. 
The best way to mark mandatory form fields is by including the information within the form labels as all users read the label before filling up form details.
suggestion: Provide the instruction explaining the meaning of the asterisk (*) such as * denotes mandatory fields. Place the instruction at the top of the form to ensure all users can access the instructions.

rationale: Valid HTML mark-up is essential for the website content to be displayed accurately across different browsers. In the absence of valid HTML mark-up, some web browsers may not render the content correctly or present a correct object model to assistive technology. Therefore, it is essential that mark-up of all the pages is valid.
suggestion:
Ensure that all pages have a valid mark-up. However, to meet accessibility compliance, it is necessary that the following three main requirements are met:
 All elements are nested properly.
 All elements have starting and ending tags.
 Within each page, the values of the id attributes are unique.

rationale:
Images are defined through CSS to reduce the page load time. However, only decorative images that do not convey information must be defined through CSS. Alternate text needs to be specified for informative images and the alternate text cannot be specified through CSS.

Moreover, users browsing the website with Windows High Contrast setting turned on will not be able to view these images as images defined through CSS are not visible when the Windows High Contrast mode is turned on.
suggestion: Implement the image using element and provide empty alt attribute (alt="") in its source code as hidden textual description "close" is provided for the image. rationale: All the page functionality should be accessible with different input devices used by the users. Lack of keyboard access makes it difficult for users with visual and mobility impairments to access web page functionality.

issue: Non-modal dialog available only on mouse hover
description:
Keyboard support is missing for the Non-modal dialog content that becomes available when users hover over "SVK" image links in the main content area.
This made it difficult for keyboard-only and screen reader users to access the Non-modal dialog content.
rationale:
Keyboard-only users when interact with a Non-modal dialog, can view the Non-modal dialog on the screen. However, if there focus fails to move to the Non-modal dialog content they are required to tab pass all the interactive functionality of the original page before they can move on the Non-modal dialog. This will result in frustration for keyboard-only users while interacting with the web page. 
When web page content gets updated dynamically, ensure that the users focus moves to the updated section. This will help users access the Non-modal dialog content without requiring to go through the content displayed on the original page from which they accessed the Non-modal dialog.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 Modify the scripts to ensure that the Non-modal dialog content is available on focus as well as on hover. onfocus and onblur keyboard handlers can be used to ensure that the Non-modal dialog is available for keyboard only users. 
 Specify key listeners to make sure that the Non-modal dialog becomes visible when users press the Tab and Up/Down Arrow keys.
 Add a hidden instruction for the benefit of screen reader users to inform them about the presence of the Non-modal dialog. Add an instruction, such as "Press the tab key to access the Pop up content. This instruction should be placed before the image link in the page source.
In the page source code, move the Non-modal dialog content immediately after the image link that triggers it. 
Add the text "dialog content starts" and "dialog content ends" at the beginning and at the end of the Non-modal dialog content to assist blind users. Position this text off-screen via CSS.

issue: Links to PDF not indicative enough
description: Some instances of links were found that open a PDF file upon clicking. For example, links such as "SVK" and "SVK" load a PDF document when activated. However, the users were not informed about the PDF in advance. In addition, information about the size of the PDF file was not made available for users. Code Snippet: Petco Code of Ethics
rationale: Links that unexpectedly open PDF documents may be a source of confusion for some users, such as users of screen readers. Moreover, specifying the file size helps users (especially users browsing with a low bandwidth connection) to decide if they wish to access the file. Thus, it is advisable to identify the file type and size for alternate format documents made available on the website to help screen reader users, users with cognitive impairments and low bandwidth users to access the information with ease.
suggestion: Inform users about links displaying information in alternate file format, such as PDF along with the file size. This will help users decide if they wish to access the information made available in alternate formats, such as PDF etc.

issue: Content not available with browser's Zoom-Text Only
description: When low vision users view the page after zooming in using the browsers zoom text only option, some content in the main content area is not available. As  a result, low vision users cannot access the mentioned content.
rationale: Flexible layouts should be used to display the page content. Dimensions for container elements used to display the page content should be defined using relative units, such as "%", "em", "rem" etc. to ensure that the content fits on the page when zoomed in. If content is not available when users zoom in, low vision users cannot access the content.
suggestion: Ensure that the page content is displayed using relative units for text and container so that users can access the page with zoom settings turned on. At the minimum, the page layouts should support up to 200% zoom.

issue: Insufficient color contrast for informative images
description:
Some informative images with insufficient color contrast were found: 
 The #858585 "SVK" Informative image in the main content area has insufficient color contrast ratio of 1.5:1 with #66B2C8 background.
 The #323232 "SVK" informative image in the footer area has insufficient color contrast ratio of 1.8:1 with #146075 background.
rationale:
All the non text content of a web page including informative images, user interface controls and their different states such as hover, focus,active,etc. should be displayed using sufficient contrast between foreground/background and adjacent colors to make sure that people with low vision and those with color deficiencies can access them.
WCAG 2.1 has outlined the contrast requirement for non text content as 3:1.
suggestion: For all informative images, ensure that the contrast between foreground, background or adjacent colors is 3:1 to make the image's information accessible for users with low vision and color-blindness.

issue: Purpose of input field not identified programmatically
description: The purpose of "SVK", "SVK", etc. input fields is not identified programmatically in "SVK" form. This will result in users with learning and mobility disabilities taking longer to fill in the details as they had to type the details instead of the values getting filled automatically.
rationale:
Web forms that collect user information should have their purpose defined programmatically. This will help user agents, such as browsers, assistive technologies etc. to access this information and present it to users. By programmatically declaring the type of data required for an input field helps users with cognitive and mobility disabilities fill out forms easily. 
Labels and form instructions do provide guidance for filling up a form. However, labels and instruction are subject to change from one web form to another. By defining the input purpose programmatically via consistent autocomplete token values, users can fill up the form details easily without requiring to memorize the details or type them on each form.
suggestion:
Use HTML 5.2 autocomplete attribute to add token values for "SVK" and "SVK" input fields. This will improve the browser's auto fill feature as well as can be used by plug-ins to fill in the details for users with mobility and cognitive impairments. For more information on how to implement this can be found at:
https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Techniques/html/H98

description: As soon as users press the left mouse button down on "SVK", "SVK" and "SVK" drop-down lists in the SVK form, the options become visible. Similarly, the drop-down options become visible when users touch the arrow icon on the mobile environments. This will create problems for users who have unintentially touched the dropdown and have no means to cancel the activation.
rationale: User interface controls should get activated when users press and release the left mouse button or when they lift their figure after touching on an user interface element. If user interface element gets activated before user releases the the left mouse button or lifts their figure on a touch device, they might unintentionally activate the element. This can lead to frustration for users with learning and mobility impairments.
suggestion: User interface elements should get activated on up-event and not on down-event.

rationale: Textual labels of user interface elements should have accessible names that match with their visual labels. Accessible names that are defined programmatically in the mark-up are rendered by assistive technologies, such as screen readers, speech recognition systems, text-to-speech systems for their users. Speech recognition users will look at the visual label and give command to their assistive technology. If accessible name and visual label of an user interface element do not match, users will find it difficult to give  commands to their assistive technologies. Similarly, text-to-speech users will have an indifferent reading experience when visual label and accessible names do not match.
suggestion: It is recommended to specify accessible names for user interface elements that matches their visual label. Specify accessible names as "SVK", "SVK", etc. for "SVK", "SVK", etc. links. Edit (...) Edit

issue: Content not accessible for low vision users
description: Content that becomes available when users hover over with a mouse  on "SVK" button is not accessible for low vision users. When low vision users try to move their mouse on the content in order to read it, the content disappears. Thus low vision users found it difficult to access the content.
rationale: Low vision users access web page content at high magnification levels. At high magnification levels, all the page's content is not visible on the screen for users and they have to scroll in order to access the content. Information that is displayed when users hover over an element or information that is displayed when user's focus moves on an element should remain visible on the screen until user dismisses it. As low vision users will try to move their mouse or keyboard focus to the content displayed on hover or focus and as soon as their pointer moves from the element the content is no longer visible. This makes it difficult for low vision users to access the content.
suggestion: Modify the scripts associated with the tooltip content to ensure that it remains visible until user decides to dismiss the tooltip. This will enable low vision users to navigate to the tooltip content and dismiss it after accessing it.

issue: Loading message not announced for screen reader users
description: The loading icon that gets displayed on the page when users click "SVK" button is not rendered by screen readers. Thus  screen reader users are not aware about "loading" message being displayed on the page.
rationale: Status messages are often used on the web to convey important information to the users. These messages are either displayed in text or even in the form of icons and images. Different type of status messages include information about errors occurred on a page, successful form submission, results of actions taken by users, progress of a task etc. These messages often get dynamically added on the page resulting in screen reader users going unaware about their presence on the page. Therefore it is essential that whenever a status message gets added on the page, it is made available for screen reader users.
suggestion: Specify ARIA role "status" and set aria-live as "polite" for the loading message. This will ensure that screen readers will identify the message for their users as soon as it is displayed on the page.

description: Upper portion of the words "SVK" in "SVK" dropdown list gets cut off when users try to access the page with text spacing styles turned on. This will result in users with low vision and users with learning impairments finding it difficult to read the text.
rationale:
Fixed dimensions used for text spacing, line spacing, paragraph spacing by web page authors can not be readable for users with low vision or users with learning impairments. These users have a tendency to browse the web using custom style sheets where spacing is defined to meet their needs. A web page should not override user defined styles and thereby make it possible for users to access the content with ease. If the web page overrides user styles, it might become difficult for users to access the content. 
When user styles are applied, text on a web page should not get cut off vertically, overlap with other piece of text and even not remain fixed. Text should be displayed as per the user styles to ensure that users with learning impairments and low vision users can access the content with ease.
suggestion: Define the text and container sizes using percent (%), em and rem units. In addition use relative units  for defining CSS line-height, letter-spacing etc. properties and ensure that user's text spacing styles are not overridden.

issue: Processing request message not announced for screen reader users
description: The "SVK" message that gets displayed on the page when users click on "SVK" link is not rendered by screen readers. Thus screen reader users are not aware about "SVK" message being displayed on the page.
rationale: Status messages are often used on the web to convey important information to the users. These messages are either displayed in text or even in the form of icons and images. Different type of status messages include information about errors occurred on a page, successful form submission, results of actions taken by users, progress of a task etc. These messages often get dynamically added on the page resulting in screen reader users going unaware about their presence on the page. Therefore it is essential that whenever a status message gets added on the page, it is made available for screen reader users.
suggestion: Specify ARIA role "status" for "SVK" message. This will ensure that screen readers will identify the message for their users as soon as it is displayed on the page.

description:
Keyboard support is missing for the content that becomes available when users hover the text in the main content area of the page. 
For example, content such as "SVK", "SVK", etc. is displayed only when users hover the mouse on text such as "SVK", "SVK", etc. This made it difficult for keyboard-only and screen reader users to access the functionality associated with it.
rationale: All the page functionality should be accessible with different input devices used by the users. Users should be able to access the page functionality with pointing device, such as a mouse, a keyboard or any other input device. Users with visual impairments and mobility impairments use a keyboard to access the internet. Lack of keyboard access makes it difficult for users with visual and mobility impairments to access web page functionality.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 Modify the scripts to ensure that the contnet is available on focus as well as on hover. onfocus and onblur keyboard handlers can be used to ensure that the link is available for keyboard only users. 
 Specify key listeners to make sure that the content becomes visible when users press the Tab and Up/Down Arrow keys.
 Add a hidden instruction for the benefit of screen reader users to inform them about the presence of the content. Add an instruction, such as "Press the tab key to access the content. This instruction should be placed before "SVK", "SVK", etc. text in the page source.

issue: Content not available for screen reader users
rationale: When content is not available for screen reader users, the screen reader users cannot access the information conveyed by the content.
suggestion: It is recommended to specify the information as plain text and use styles for formatting effects.

issue: No means to control moving content
description: Moving content was present below "SVK" section in the main content area of the page, but no means was provided for users to Stop/Pause and Play the moving content.
rationale: Moving content is often used on web pages to attract users attention. However, users should be provided with an option to control the movement as it might be found distracting by users with learning impairments. If moving content and static content are provided, people with attention deficit disorders find it difficult to concentrate while reading the static content of the page.
suggestion:
Ensure the movement stops automatically after five seconds.
Alternatively, provide users with an option to control the moving content through start, stop and pause buttons or links.

issue: Content not available on mobile device
description: The "SVK" content such as the several blockquotes present in the main content area are not available on portrait and landscape mode. As a result mobile users will miss out important information on the screen.
rationale:
When the screen orientation is not restricted to portrait mode, users with dexterity impairments, who have mounted their device on a wheelchair will be able to use the content in their preferred  orientation. Moreover, low vision users will be able to view content in the landscape orientation.
When screen orientation is fixed to either landscape or portrait mode, users with dexterity impairments, and low vision users may find it difficult to access the app effectively.
suggestion: Use CSS media queries to ensure that all the page content is available in both landscape and portrait orientation. Use media query "@media screen and (orientation: portrait)" to define how the mentioned table will be displayed in both the orientations.

issue: Non-Modal dialog inaccessible for keyboard-only and screen reader users
description:
On the pages, when the "SVK" checkbox is checked, a non-modal dialog gets displayed on the screen. The following issues were found with regards to the non-modal dialog:
 Focus directly moves on the "SVK" button for screen reader users when the Non-modal dialog is displayed
 Screen reader users are not aware of the Non-modal dialog. In fact, blind users are not informed that a Non-modal dialog is displayed on the page, and they have no means to distinguish between the Non-modal dialog content and the content of the page behind it.
rationale:
Keyboard-only users when interact with a Non-modal dialog, can view the Non-modal dialog on the screen. However, if there focus fails to move to the Non-modal dialog content they are required to tab pass all the interactive functionality of the original page before they can move on the Non-modal dialog. This will result in frustration for keyboard-only users while interacting with the web page. 
When web page content gets updated dynamically, ensure that the users focus moves to the updated section. This will help users access the Non-modal dialog content without requiring to go through the content displayed on the original page from which they accessed the Non-modal dialog.
suggestion:
We recommend the following changes be applied:
 In the page source code, move the non-modal dialog content immediately after the link that triggers it. 
 When the Non-modal dialog is displayed, set the keyboard focus to the beginning of its content (tabindex="-1" can be used for this purpose).
 Add the text dialog content starts and dialog content ends at the beginning and at the end of the non-modal dialog content to assist blind users. Position this text off-screen via CSS.
 Additionally, Provide a textual captcha that can be solved by all the users. For example, simple text questions can be added.

issue: Content not readout properly for Screen reader users
description:
Content related to "SVK" information is not read out properly by a screen reader. Following issues were identified:
 The "SVK" information in the modal dialog (that becomes available on activating the "SVK" interactive element  > SVK), is announced inappropriately by screen reader. For example, the "SVK" information of two different team is read out all together. As a result, screen reader users cannot access the information effectively. 
 The #F29766 and #4EC9B0 color is used to identify the "SVK" and "SVK" respectively. Users with color deficiencies will not be able to differentiate between the colors.
 Additionally, these colors are defined through CSS background color property. As a result, these colors are not available for users browsing the website with the Windows High Contrast Mode turned on.
rationale:
Users with visual impairments, such as color-blind users and blind users will find it difficult to understand information that is conveyed using color alone. When color is used to convey important information, it is essential that information is also available in at least one additional form, such as patterns, symbols, text etc. 
Information conveyed with the help of color is easy to understand for people with learning disabilities. However, if color alone is used people with visual impairments will find it difficult to understand the information. Thus, it is essential to supplement color with symbols, text, patterns etc.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 In the mentioned example along with color, use additional visual clues (e.g. different patterns, borders, etc.) to display the stats of different teams.
 Provide the information in the image format and specify an empty alt attribute for the image. This will ensure that screen reader skips the image.
 Provide Disclosure (Show/Hide) button which includes detailed information in a tabular format for the benefit of screen reader users. Position the button next to the image to maintain the presentation of the web page as well as make it easier for users to find it.

description:

This made it difficult for keyboard-only and screen reader users to access the functionality effectively.
rationale: All the page functionality should be accessible with different input devices used by the users. Users should be able to access the page functionality with pointing device, such as a mouse, a keyboard or any other input device. Users with visual impairments and mobility impairments use a keyboard to access the internet. Lack of keyboard access makes it difficult for users with visual and mobility impairments to access web page functionality.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:

 Modify the scripts to ensure that the buttons are accessible with a keyboard as well as a mouse to make the functionality accessible for keyboard-only and screen reader users.
 Provide unique and descriptive label such as SVK, SVK, SVK, etc. for the buttons via aria-label attribute.
 Provide hidden message as SVK. Specify role="status" and set aria-live as "polite" for the hidden message. This will ensure that screen readers will identify the message for their users as soon as 4 teams are  selected.

issue: Custom drop-down inaccessible for screen reader users
description: Custom drop-down lists, such as "SVK", "SVK", etc. are included on the page that enables screen users to select an option "SVK", "SVK", "SVK", "SVK", "SVK", "SVK","SVK","SVK", etc. respectively . However, the drop-down lists are inaccessible for screen reader users.
rationale: All the interactive elements must be accessible for assistive technology users such as screen reader users. When native HTML elements are used basic information about an element, such as name, role, state and value is exposed to assistive technologies by default. However, for custom components this information needs to be made available for assistive technologies so users can interact with the custom component effectively.
suggestion:

More information on how to achieve this can be found at:
https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria-practices/examples/combobox/aria1.1pattern/listbox-combo.html

issue: Text gets overlapped and cut off
description: The content such as "SVK" and "SVK" gets overlapped and the text "SVK" gets cut off when users try to access the page with text spacing styles turned on. This will result in users with low vision and users with learning impairments finding it difficult to read the text.
rationale:
Fixed dimensions used for text spacing, line spacing, paragraph spacing by web page authors can not be readable for users with low vision or users with learning impairments. These users have a tendency to browse the web using custom style sheets
 where spacing is defined to meet their needs. A web page should not override user defined styles and thereby make it possible for users to access the content with ease. If the web page overrides user styles, it might become difficult for users to access the
 content.
When user styles are applied, text on a web page should not get cut off vertically, overlap with other piece of text and even not remain fixed. Text should be displayed as per the user styles to ensure that users with learning impairments and low vision users
 can access the content with ease.
suggestion:
Define the text and container sizes using percent (%), em and rem units.
In addition use relative units  for defining CSS line-height, letter-spacing etc. properties and ensure that user's text spacing styles are not overridden.
