issue: PDF - Decorative images are not artifacted in PDF
description: Decorative image is identified on the tested document. This resulted in visually impaired users being able to access the image.
rationale:
Alternate text is provided to help users understand the information conveyed through an image. Alternate text is required by users with visual impairments to understand the purpose of the image. In the absence of an appropriate alternate text,
 screen reader users will find it difficult to interpret the image information.
If images are used for decorative purpose, then artifact the image to ensure that the screen reader skips the images.
suggestion: Artifact all the decorative images to ensure that the screen reader skips the decorative images.

description:
Inappropriate title was specified as "SVK" for the PDF document. Additionally, the document title was not set.
This made it difficult for screen reader users to identify the type of information made available in the pdf.
rationale: Title is the first piece of information that a screen reader reads when the file loads. Title should be meaningful and descriptive for screen reader users to understand the document content. Unique and descriptive title should be specified for each PDF file.
suggestion:
Provide appropriate, meaningful and descriptive title for the PDF file. The title can be set under File > Properties > Description > Title in Adobe Acrobat.

To set the initial view, select "Document Title" option from the "show" drop down list available in the "Initial View" tab of the "Document Properties" dialog.

description: The document was checked for errors using Adobe Acrobat Professionals Accessibility Full Check utility and it generated "Tagged PDF - Failed", "Primary Language - Failed", "Title - Failed", "Tagged content - Failed", etc. errors.
rationale:
Adobe Acrobat Professional provides with an option to check a document for Accessibility errors. By solving this error, authors can ensure that document is accessible for all users including screen reader users.
A document with no errors is more likely to be accessible then a document with errors.
suggestion: Check the document using Accessibility Full Check utility and ensure that it has no errors by tagging the content appropriately.

issue: PDF - Content untagged in PDF document
description: The document was untagged. A dialog box stating Reading Untagged document appears for screen reader users as soon as they open the document.
rationale: PDF document content should be tagged in a way that the structural information is provided to the users. When a PDF document is not tagged, the content is not available properly for screen reader users.
suggestion:
All the content of a document including text, decorative lines, images and even blank spaces must be tagged using appropriate tags. Tag the document to ensure that screen reader users do not get this dialog box and are able to access the content effectively.

To provide a tag structure, authors can either,
 Recreate the document with a tag structure or;
 Manually add the tag structure using the Adobe Acrobat "Add Tags..." feature under the "Advanced - Accessibility" menu item in Acrobat.

issue: PDF - Language of the document not specified
description: Language of the document was not specified for the PDF document.
rationale: User agents can provide content more accurately when the language of a PDF document is identified. For instance, screen readers can load the right pronunciation and PDF Readers can display characters and scripts correctly. Screen readers and Braille displays might interpret and convey the information incorrectly if the language is not specified. Document language helps assistive technologies identify the language of the content and convey content information to the users.
suggestion: Use "Document Properties" to define the language of the document. Choose file > properties > advanced tab > Language.

description: List not defined for the related content across the tested pages of PDF document. For instance on page 1, the content such as  "SVK", "SVK", etc. are not tagged using list markup.
rationale:
Lists are used to display content in a simpler format. Screen readers such as JAWS inform the users about the beginning/end of a list and number of items in a list. 
Appropriate mark-up enables assistive technologies to pass on the list information to the user.
suggestion: Use the <L>, <LI>, <Lbl> and <LBody> tags to define lists in a PDF document.

description: Heading not defined across the tested pages of PDF document. For instance on page 1, the content such as "SVK." is not tagged as heading.
rationale: Headings are used to add structure to the document. Appropriate heading structure enables screen reader users to understand the document structure. Use headings in the correct sequence; such as h2 should follow h1, h3 should follow h2 and so on. Screen reader users have a tendency to skim through the document using heading level navigation. Appropriate heading structure enables screen reader users to understand the document structure as well as navigate through the document easily.
suggestion: Use appropriate heading levels by manualling adding (H1-H6) tag to the Tag tree and placing the on-screen heading text under this element.

description: The PDF was an untagged document. As a result, document structure of the PDF file is inaccurate.
rationale: Document structure is very essential for assistive technology users. On the basis of document structure, assistive technologies such as screen readers will read the content for their users. A logical document structure will ensure that content is read out in a meaningful sequence for screen reader users. In the absence of logical document structure, screen reader users will find it difficult to interpret the content as the content might not be read out in a correct sequence.
suggestion:
To set the document structure apply the following steps:
1. Select Page Thumbnails from left panel.
2. Right click on the Page Thumbnails and select Page Properties.
3. Choose "Use Document Structure"  from the options.

description: The page content failed to reflow when viewed after turning on "Reflow" option of Adobe Acrobat Reader. This will result in low vision users finding it difficult to access the content in the reflow mode.
rationale:
Low vision users access content by using Adobe Acrobat Reader's reflow option turned on. The content in the reflow mode gets reflowed and is displayed in a single column.
Low vision users find it very helpful to access content displayed in a single column as they do not have to scroll both ways; horizontally and vertically in order to read it. If the page content fails to reflow when the reflow option is turned on, it becomes very difficult for low vision users to access the content. The content should not get overlapped and should not get cut off in the reflow mode and the content should reflow and displayed in a single column. Additionally, all the content should be available for the users.
suggestion: Tag the content correctly to ensure that the reading order of the content is logical. This will ensure that the content is reflowed correctly in the reflow mode.

description: Bookmarks was not defined for the document. This made it difficult for users with visual impairments to navigate to the respective section of the document quickly.
rationale: Bookmarks are specified to enable users navigate to different sections of the document quickly. Bookmarks are mainly beneficial for users with visual impairments as they rely on them to navigate the document.
suggestion: It is recommended to add bookmarks to enable users with visual impairments navigate to different sections of the document quickly and easily.

description: Images was found across PDF tested pages except on page 3, 9 and 17 which were not tagged. As a result, screen reader fails to identify the images.
rationale: PDF document content should be tagged in a way that the content is provided to the users. If the content is not tagged, it may not be available for screen reader users.
suggestion: Tag the images with <figure> tag and provide alternate text for the  image.

issue: PDF - Missing table markup in the PDF document
description: Data tables was found on page 31 and 33 which were not tagged. This made it difficult for screen reader to access the table effectively.
rationale: Data tables provide content in certain context. When the screen reader users are informed about a table, they expect for table headers and data cells. Navigating through each cell enables them to identify and interpret tabular content. Appropriate table tags should be used to display tabular information to help users with visual impairments interpret the information easily.
suggestion:
Mark the data tables using <Table>, <TR>, <TH> and <TD> tags. Mark table headers such as "Permutations", "Combinations" with <TH> tag.

Set the scope attribute on the <TH> tag of each table header and assign either the name "Column", "Row" or "Both" to the attribute.

For Complex Tables - Provides ID and Headers array to associate header cells to data cells. A unique ID attribute should be set on each header cells. The Headers associated with a <TD> tag should be set to the IDs that correspond to header cells.

issue: PDF - List and nested list not defined in the PDF document
description:
Several instances were found wherein the content displayed as a list visually was not identified as a list for screen reader users in the PDF file.
For example, on page 2 the following information is not defined using list tags:
(i) SVK
(ii) SVK
(iii) SVK
(iv) SVK
(v) SVK
rationale:
Lists are used to display content in a simpler format. Screen readers such as JAWS inform the users about the beginning/end of a list and number of items in a list. 
Appropriate mark-up enables assistive technologies to pass on the list information to the user.
suggestion: Use the <L>, <LI>, <Lbl> and <LBody> tags to define lists in a PDF document.

issue: PDF - Color alone is used to convey information in PDF document
description: Colors are used to convey the information in "SVK" image. Users who suffer from color deficiencies will not be able to differentiate between the colors and will have no way to easily identify the information.
rationale:
Users with visual impairments, such as colorblind users and blind users will find it difficult to understand information that is conveyed using color alone. When color is used to convey important information, it is essential that information is also available in at least one additional form, such as patterns, symbols, text etc. 
Information conveyed with the help of color is easy to understand for people with learning disabilities. However, if color alone is used people with visual impairments will find it difficult to understand the information. Thus, it is essential to supplement color with symbols, text, patterns etc.
suggestion: Use additional visual cues (e.g. different patterns) to indicate the information of the image.

issue: PDF - Image of text used in the PDF document
description: Images of text were used to display important information. For instance, the text such as "SVK" on  page 6 was displayed using image of text.
rationale: Often text is created as an image to enhance the presentation of the page content. However, text when created as an image, makes it difficult for low vision users to read the information. Text created as an image becomes pixelated and blur when zoomed in. Thus it becomes difficult for users with low vision who use screen magnifiers to access the page content.
suggestion: Use of images of text should be avoided (except for logo types, maps, diagrams etc.) to ensure that the text does not become pixilated when zoomed in. Thus it is recommended to specify the information as plain text and use styles for formatting effects.

description:
The keyboard and screen reader user were unable to identify the links on page 1 and page 2. It appeared as simple text content rather than actual link.
For example, following are instances of page 1:
 SVK
 SVK
 SVK
rationale: Appropriate link tags enable screen readers to identify the presence of links and enable users to activate links in the PDF document. It also helps other keyboard users to use the link in the PDF document.
suggestion: Links need to be tagged using <Link> tag with appropriate Link-OBJR property. The <Link> tag should be used to include the link text and Link-OBJR property is used to include the reference URL.

description: The footnotes present in the PDF document were not tagged. This resulted in visually impaired users being unable to access the related content.
rationale: Footnotes are an additional piece of information available at the bottom of a page. Appropriate tags enable screen reader user to identify the related information.
suggestion:
Use <reference> tag for the footnote and <note> tag for the footnote content. Additionally, provide footnote in the "Actual text" input field option in "Object Properties window".
Apply the following to provide actual text
(i) Right-click on the footnote tag in the tags tree
(ii) Choose Properties
(iii) In the object properties window, provide actual text

issue: PDF - Decorative images are not artifacted
description: Decorative image is identified on the tested document. This resulted in visually impaired users being able to access the image.
rationale:
Alternate text is provided to help users understand the information conveyed through an image. Alternate text is required by users with visual impairments to understand the purpose of the image. In the absence of an appropriate alternate text, screen reader users will find it difficult to interpret the image information.
If images are used for decorative purpose, then artifact the image to ensure that the screen reader skips the images.
suggestion: Artifact all the decorative images to ensure that the screen reader skips the decorative images.

description:
The page numbers in the PDF document are not consistent with the page numbers displayed in the PDF viewer page controls. As a result, assistive technology user
will find it difficult to locate the page in the document.
For example, the page number in the document are specified as "1", "2", "3" etc. whereas those displayed in the Adobe Reader page navigation toolbar are "1" for all the 3 pages and "2" for the remaining 3 pages.
rationale:
Page numbers displayed in the PDF viewer page controls should have the same page numbers as the document. Consistency in presenting the document's
page numbers will help make navigating the document more predictable and understandable. Some documents include page number as roman numbers, different patterns of numbering is followed in front matter of a document, core content etc.  
If consistent page numbers are not provided, assistive technology users will find it difficult to locate the page in the document.
suggestion:
Ensure that the page number in the document is identical as in the page number available in PDF viewer page controls.
Tag the page numbers as "1", "2", etc. respectively in the PDF document.

issue: Color alone used to distinguish links when contrast requirement is met
description: Color alone #247EA8 is used to distinguish "SVK", "SVK", "SVK", etc. links from the surrounding text #11111. Even though sufficient color contrast is provided the links does not have persistent underline making it difficult for users with color blindness to distinguish the link within the running text.
rationale: Users with color deficiencies may not be able to quickly identify links on the website. This is especially true for links displayed within paragraphs when color alone is used to differentiate links from the surrounding text. Therefore it is essential to underline links persistently or make sure that the underline appears when the links receive keyboard focus.
suggestion: To make it easy for all users to recognize links the best practice is to persistently underline them. Ensure that the underline appears not only when the mouse is hovered over the links but also when the links receive keyboard focus.

description: The date buttons such as "SVK", "SVK","SVK", etc. are not identified as buttons visually for low vision (ZoomText) users. It creates difficulty for ZoomText users to identify and access the buttons.
rationale: On the web, designers often make use of different fonts, font styles, font sizes to display textual information for both interactive and non-interactive elements to their users. It becomes difficult for low vision users who use screen magnifiers to access web content to read the non-interactive elements and to access the interactive elements.
suggestion: Ensure that the mentioned buttons visually look like buttons so that ZoomText users can identify and access the buttons.

description:
Title was not specified for the PDF document. Additionally, the document title was not set.

This made it difficult for screen reader users to identify the type of information made available in the pdf.
rationale: Title is the first piece of information that a screen reader reads when the file loads. Title should be meaningful and descriptive for screen reader users to understand the document content. Unique and descriptive title should be specified for each PDF file.
suggestion:
Provide appropriate, meaningful and descriptive title for the PDF file. The title can be set under File > Properties > Description > Title in Adobe Acrobat.

To set the initial view, select "Document Title" option from the "show" drop down list available in the "Initial View" tab of the "Document Properties" dialog.

issue: PDF - Missing text alternative for informative image
description: The Text alternative is not specified for the logo image. In the absence of text alternative, screen reader identified the image as random numbers.
rationale: Alternate text is provided to help users understand the information conveyed through an image. Alternate text is required by users with visual impairments to understand the purpose of the image. In the absence of an appropriate alternate text, screen reader users will find it difficult to interpret the image information.
suggestion: Provide appropriate alternate text for informative images. For example, provide alternate text for the logo image as SVK on page 2.

issue: Content read out repetitively by screen readers
description: The content such as "SVK" and "SVK"  in the main content area should be hidden. Since link for "SVK" is provided, the table information is read out twice which was found confusing by screen reader users to understand.
rationale:
When content is read out by screen readers repetitively, users will get confused and might find the information irrelevant as they have not tried to access the content twice.
Users with visual impairments rely on assistive technologies such as screen readers to access the page content. A screen reader  reads the content in a linear order as it is included in the page source. If the information is read out repetitively , then screen reader users will get confused as they have not tried to access the information again.
suggestion: Hide the mentioned content from screen reader users by specifying aria-hidden = true in the page source.

issue: Sort functionality not operable with a keyboard
description:
On the page, keyboard support is missing for SVK functionality present in the table.
This made it difficult for keyboard-only and screen reader users to access the sort functionality.
rationale: All the page functionality should be accessible with different input devices used by the users. Users should be able to access the page functionality with pointing device, such as a mouse, a keyboard or any other input device. Users with visual impairments and mobility impairments use a keyboard to access the internet. Lack of keyboard access makes it difficult for users with visual and mobility impairments to access web page functionality.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 Add role="grid" for <table> element.
 Include the "SVK" image and the "SVK" text in a single <div> element instead of separate <div> elements and use CSS if necessary to maintain the presentation.
 Add aria-sort="ascending" attribute for <th> element. Ensure that the value of the 'aria-sort' attribute changes on user interaction via scripting.
 Add role="button" and tabindex="0" attribute for <div> element containing "SVK" image and the "SVK" text.
 Ensure that the "SVK" image is present  for all the sortable columns of the table.

issue: Note: Separator announced by screen reader
description: The screen reader announces "separator" for the horizontal bar after "SVK" drop-down list as it is defined using <hr> tag.
suggestion: The horizontal bar should be defined using CSS instead of <hr> tag to ensure that it is not identified by a screen reader. Define a border via CSS class to include a horizontal line separator. Alternatively, specify role="presentation" to <hr> tag.

issue: Sub-menu links displayed on keyboard focus
description:
When "SVK", "SVK", "SVK", etc. menus receive the keyboard focus, the respective sub-menu links get displayed automatically.
As a result, keyboard-only and screen reader users will have to navigate through the sub-menu links even when not required.
Additionally, the sub-menu links of "SVK" menu are announced by the screen reader as users access the content using arrow keys even when the sub-menu links are not visible.
rationale:
When sub-menu links get displayed automatically on keyboard focus, the keyboard-only and screen reader users have to navigate through the sub-menu links forcefully.
The sub-menu links should get displayed / removed when a menu is activated.
When hidden content is read out by screen readers, users will get confused and might find the information irrelevant as they have not tried to access the hidden content.
suggestion:
Apply the following changes:
 Ensure that the sub-menu links do not get displayed automatically when the menus receive the keyboard focus.
 Ensure that the sub-menu links get expanded / collapsed on activating  "SVK" menu.
 Provide a image button besides "SVK", "SVK", etc. menu links that expands / collapses the sub-menu links on user interaction.
 Remove "aria-expanded" and "aria-haspopup" attributes from <a> element of "SVK", "SVK", etc. menu links.
 Set aria-expanded attribute to false for the image buttons by default as the image buttons are in the collapsed state. Ensure that the value of aria-expanded attribute gets updated on user interaction via scripting.
 Ensure that the content that should not be available to users, is hidden through the CSS display:none property rather than positioning it off-screen via CSS.

description:
Inappropriate label "SVK" is provided for the SVK button in the modal dialog (that becomes available when the page loads).
As a result, screen reader users might not understand the purpose of the button effectively.

Code Snippet:
<button title="Close (Esc)" type="button" class="mfp-close">×</button>
rationale:
Assistive technologies, such as screen readers identify each form control on the basis of their label. This is found very useful by users as it not only helps them to understand the purpose of the form control but also helps to differentiate one form control from another. 
Form control label enables users to understand the purpose of the form control. Inappropriate form control label makes it difficult for the screen reader users to understand the functionality associated to the form control effectively.
suggestion:
Provide appropriate label "SVK" for the button  using "aria-label" attribute.

<button title="Close (Esc)" type="button" class="mfp-close" aria-label="Close">×</button>

description:
The "SVK" field is identified as mandatory using "required" attribute.
However, "SVK" field is not mandatory. This might create confusion for screen reader users.

Code Snippet:
<input id="fileupload" class="fileupload" type="file" file-model-apply="fileFormName" custom-on-change="assignDocumentDetails" required="">
rationale:
Screen reader users often prefer identifying mandatory form field information in advance to complete the form successfully and avoid error messages. Well-defined mandatory form fields are also helpful for users with mobility impairments as they can fill up the form with ease. In the absence of mandatory form field information, users might incorrectly fill up the form and thus might lead to frustration. 
The best way to mark mandatory form fields is by including the information within the form labels as all users read the label before filling up the form details.  
When fields are identified as mandatory inappropriately, screen reader users might get confused.
suggestion:
Remove "required" attribute from the source code of the field.

<input id="fileupload" class="fileupload" type="file" file-model-apply="fileFormName" custom-on-change="assignDocumentDetails">
